ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for Lamb Shank Nihari; mapped to broader 'lamb dish' class. A more specific term for slow-braised South Asian lamb shank stew is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Nihari. A00GJ (meat-based dishes, lamb/mutton stew) is the closest defensible analogous category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Lamb Shank Nihari or equivalent South Asian lamb stew preparation.

main_ingredients: nihari masala (fennel, dry ginger, long pepper, cardamom, coriander, cumin, cloves, bay leaf)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Lamb Shank Nihari is approximately 350 g, comprising a bone-in lamb shank piece with a generous ladle of the spiced, collagen-rich gravy, typically served in a deep bowl alongside naan or sheermal bread.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Lamb Shank Nihari exists in ICMR-NIN tables. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for slow-cooked mutton/lamb curry with bone, adjusted for the higher fat content of shank cuts and the small quantity of whole wheat flour used as a thickener. USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, Item 17174 — Lamb, shank, cooked, braised) was used as a cross-reference for protein, fat, and micronutrient plausibility. Values are consistent with both sources.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lamb Shank Nihari. GI is estimated at 30 (low) based on the analogous profile of slow-cooked meat-dominant stews with minimal carbohydrate contribution. The primary carbohydrate source is a small quantity of whole wheat flour (atta) used as a thickener; whole wheat flour has a GI of approximately 69 (University of Sydney GI Database), but its contribution per 100 g of the finished dish is negligible (approximately 2–3 g available carbohydrate from flour). The overall dish GI is dominated by the very low glycemic response of meat, fat, and spices. GL per serving (350 g) = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (30 × 14.7) / 100 ≈ 4.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (350 mg/100 g; ~1225 mg per 350 g serving) and significant saturated fat load from lamb shank and cooking fat
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Lamb Shank Nihari to small, infrequent portions (≤150 g), request reduced-salt preparation, skim visible fat from the surface of the gravy, and avoid pairing with high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or salted bread.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~30) and high protein content (16.5 g/100 g) support glycemic control; minimal carbohydrate load from the dish itself
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lamb Shank Nihari is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions (approximately 200–250 g of stew) when paired with a small serving of whole-grain bread rather than refined naan, and total caloric intake is monitored to avoid excess energy from the high fat content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~5.8 g/100 g), estimated cholesterol (~72 mg/100 g), and high sodium contribute to cardiovascular risk when consumed in large or frequent portions
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Lamb Shank Nihari only occasionally and in small portions (≤150 g), trim all visible fat from the shank before serving, skim the fat layer from the gravy, and balance the meal with high-fibre vegetables to mitigate the saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Collagen-rich bone broth and anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, cardamom) may support joint health, but high purine content in red meat is a concern for comorbid gout
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the collagen and anti-inflammatory spice profile of Nihari, but those with comorbid gout should limit red meat intake and monitor uric acid levels.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb Shank Nihari is a slow-braised South Asian stew originating from the Mughal royal kitchens of Delhi and later popularised in Lucknow and Hyderabad. The name derives from the Arabic word 'Nahar' (morning), reflecting the tradition of cooking the dish overnight and serving it at dawn. The centrepiece is a whole bone-in lamb shank (nalli), which is braised for 6–8 hours in a sealed pot (dum method) in a deeply spiced gravy. The gravy is built on a base of caramelised onions, ginger-garlic paste, and a specialised Nihari masala containing fennel seeds, dry ginger powder, long pepper (pipli), green and black cardamom, cloves, and bay leaf. A small quantity of roasted whole wheat flour (atta) is whisked in to give the sauce its characteristic silky, slightly thickened body. The result is meat that falls effortlessly from the bone, surrounded by a broth that is intensely aromatic, rich in collagen from the marrow and connective tissue, and deeply savoury. Per 100 g, Lamb Shank Nihari provides approximately 210 kcal, 16.5 g protein, 14.8 g fat (of which ~5.8 g is saturated), and 4.2 g carbohydrate. It is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with a very low glycemic index (~30), making it suitable for glycemic control when consumed in appropriate portions. The sodium content is significant (~350 mg/100 g), which is a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is naturally halal and contains gluten from the wheat flour thickener. It is traditionally served with naan, sheermal (saffron flatbread), or kulcha, garnished with fresh ginger julienne, green chillies, coriander leaves, and a squeeze of lemon. A standard serving is approximately 350 g.
common_user_queries: What spices are in Nihari masala?
What is the glycemic index of Nihari?
meal_context: Friday special at South Asian restaurants

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Lamb Shank Nihari exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN slow-cooked mutton/lamb curry data and cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy braised lamb shank (Item 17174). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and water values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for braised lamb shank and are plausible but not directly measured for this specific preparation. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Saturated_fat_g (5.8), monounsaturated_fat_g (6.1), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.2), trans_fat_g (0.2), cholesterol_mg (72.0), and water_g (62.0) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy Item 17174 (Lamb, shank, cooked, braised) to replace -1 defaults, as ICMR-NIN does not report detailed fatty acid fractions or moisture for this preparation. These values are plausible cross-references and are documented as adapted/estimated. 2. No -1 sentinel values remain in per_100g; all fields have been populated with either measured, adapted, or estimated numeric values from approved sources. 3. The input record contained four duplicate portion entries (all '1 serving, 350g, is_default: true'); deduplicated to a single canonical serving of 350 g. 4. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Lamb Shank Nihari was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2024. 5. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (30 × 14.7 g available carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 4.4. 6. The dish contains gluten from whole wheat flour (atta) used as a thickener; not suitable for coeliac or gluten-intolerant individuals. 7. This is a Mughlai / North Indian / Hyderabadi dish; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese cuisines do not apply. Hindi and Urdu names are provided as the primary regional language aliases. Bengali alias included due to widespread consumption in Bangladesh and West Bengal.
